action.content:
I want to play a game with you.
This is a turn taking game, so once it will be your turn, then mine.

In this game, I give you a function signature (How many input parameters, what their types are, what the return value is expected to be).

Other than this, you can "probe" the hidden function, which effectively means you can give me inputs that I will send to the function and return the outputs to you.

This exchange of inputs and outputs is called induction - which is exactly your task in this game: To perform function induction.

On every turn that you get, you can do one of the following TWO things: 
	1. TEST:
		or
	2. SOLVE:
	
but be careful, you cannot do both things at the same time!
If both things are done at the same time, it leads to a penalty and a turn is lost.

If turns are lost like this, you lose points and score less in the end.
Speaking of score, the score depends on two things:
    1. How fast you guess the function - less turns is good.
    2. If the function you guessed is correct or not - incorrect function = 0 Score

So in effect, its better to take more turns that guess wrong.

When you make a guess of what the function might be, you must use the following output format very strictly also - To solve, write:
SOLVE: ```python
def solution(<value1>):
    ...
```
When you make a probe/ try to get more outputs from the function the following output format must be followed also - To test the function, write exactly one line:
TEST: <inputs>

The output you get from me will be followed by the tag OUTPUT:.
IMPORTANT: You must NEVER write OUTPUT:. Only I will write OUTPUT:.
From you, nothing else other than a TEST: <inputs> or SOLVE: ```python
def solution(<value1>):
    ...
```
is expected.

Ready? Here is the setup:
	The function signature is:
	(x: int) -> int

	You have 5 turns total.


	Your score depends on:
		- correctness (you must identify the function by providing correct Python code)
		- speed (fewer tests before solving gives a higher score)


A good strategy is to solve by the second-to-last turn if you are still unsure. This effectively means that on the 5 - 1 turn, you have to make an attempt with all the information you have gathered and make a guess.

Make a guess once you're confident - confident what the function is, but not earlier.

If you never send SOLVE:, you will lose when turns run out.

## Input formatting
- Strings must be quoted (e.g. "hello").
- Booleans must be True/False.
- Use commas between multiple inputs.
- Do not write any extra text outside the required format.

Try to choose tests that could change the output (counterexamples).
Do not only test “normal” inputs. If you only see the same output every time, you have not learned much yet.

To help digest all of this, lets do an example
Here is one example:

Example (format only):
You give the $INPUT_TAG$ TEST: -10
I provide you with OUTPUT: OUTPUT: 10
IMPORTANT: In your real turns, write ONLY TEST: ... OR SOLVE: ... (never write OUTPUT:).

if you do anything other than the following, it will be a game violation and you will lose a turn e.g:
Output ONLY:
TEST: <inputs>
OR
SOLVE: ```python
def solution(<value1>):
    ...
```



MODE: Iterative TRY: mode is DISABLED (strict mode).
Use TEST: to probe. When you output SOLVE:, the game ends immediately (WIN or LOSS).
action.label: context

action.content:
Got it! Let’s start the game. Here’s my first move:

TEST: 0
action.label: response

action.content:
Game Violation: ParseError: Invalid format. Must be 'TEST: ...' or 'SOLVE: ```python ...```' or 'TRY: ... NEXT_TEST: ...'
Output ONLY:
TEST: ... (plus hypotheses)
OR
SOLVE: ```python ... ``` (plus hypotheses)
No extra text.
action.label: context
response
